content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 28, 1994


Ruth Gilson
9308 Rose Street
Bellflower, CA 90706-6424

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-94-169

Dear Ms. Gilson:

You have requested advice concerning your reporting
obligations under the conflict of interest disclosure provisions
of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").1/ Since your letter is a
general inquiry, we consider it to be a request for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).2/

QUESTION

Are you required to report an allowance you receive for
serving as president of Lakewood/Long Beach Republican Women
Federated on your statement of economic interests (Form 721)?

CONCLUSION

If, in serving as president of Lakewood/Long Beach Republican
Women Federated, the value of your work is not equal to or greater
than the allowance paid to you, the portion of the allowance that
exceeds the value of your services is a gift. As a local elected
official, you would be required to report the value of the gift on
your statement of economic interests if it is $50 or more during
the applicable reporting period. In addition, local elected
officials cannot receive more than $1,000 per calendar year in
gifts from a single source.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Ms. Gilson
Our File No. I-94-169
Page Two

If the value of your services is equal to or greater than the
allowance, the allowance is income. Normally, income which totals
$250 or more during the applicable reporting period must be
reported on your statement of economic interests. However, if
Lakewood/Long Beach Republican Women Federated is a bona fide
educational, academic, or charitable organization, there is an
exception and you need not report income from such organizations
on your statement of economic interests.

FACTS

Pursuant to our telephone conversation on May 23, 1994, and
the facts provided in your letter, you are a councilwoman in the
City of Bellflower. You are serving as president of Lakewood/Long
Beach Republican Women Federated. You receive an allowance of $25
per month from the organization to cover miscellaneous expenses
associated with holding your position.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires certain public officials to disclose
reportable investments, interests in real property, sources of
income and business positions in their jurisdiction on a statement
of economic interests (Form 721). (Section 87200, et seq.)

The allowance you have received may be characterized as
compensation for services (income) or a gift under the Act.

Section 82030 defines "income", in part, as:

[A]ny payment received, including but not limited to any
salary, wage, advance, .... gift, .... reimbursement for
expenses, per diem, ....

Section 82028 defines a "gift" as:

[A]ny payment to the extent that consideration of equal or
greater value is not received....

If you determine that you did not provide equal or greater
consideration for the allowance, it must be disclosed as a gift on
Schedule F of your statement of economic interests. If you have
provided the organization with consideration of equal or greater
value to the value of the allowance you received, you may be
required to report the allowance as income.

Ms. Gilson
Our File No. I-94-169
Page Three


     Section 82030(b)(2) provides that "income" does not include
reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a
bona fide educational, academic or charitable organization.
Therefore, if the organization is a bona fide educational,
academic or charitable organization, the organization would not be
considered a source of income to you under the Act.

     The Commission has not defined what constitutes a bona fide
educational, academic or charitable organization in the Act.
However, the Commission has previously advised that a corporation
which is organized and operated exclusively for educational,
academic, or charitable purposes, and which is exempt from
taxation under the provisions of Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal
Revenue Code qualifies for this limited exception to the reporting
and disclosure requirements of the Act. However, not all
501(c)(3) corporations are "charitable, academic or educational,"
since that section also covers corporations that test for public
safety, foster amateur sport competitions, and engage in various
other activities. (Sonoda Advice Letter, No. A-89-179, copy
enclosed.)

     We do not have sufficient information to determine whether
your organization falls within this exemption. If the exception
applies, the allowance you receive from the organization will not
be required to be disclosed. Alternatively, if the organization
does not meet the criteria for the exemption, income you receive
from the organization of $250 or more must be disclosed on
Schedule D of your statement of economic interests.

     If you have any questions or need further assistance, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Dixie Howard
                                        Political Reform Consultant
                                        Technical Assistance Division

Enclosure
